title: Raster vs. Vector Images
concept: Raster images store a fixed grid of pixel colors, while vector images store mathematical shape instructions; machine learning models almost always work on raster grids because that grid is what a numeric array looks like.

options.text: The smallest single-color square in a digital image's grid
A unit used only to measure file size
A type of image compression algorithm

exercises.explain: Right — a pixel is one tiny colored square, and a grid of them makes up a digital image.

cards.title: Zoom in far enough and photos fall apart
cards.body: Zoom into a photo of your dog and eventually you don't see fur anymore — you see a blocky checkerboard of colored squares. Zoom into a cartoon logo and it can stay perfectly smooth no matter how close you get. Those two images are built in completely different ways.

cards.title: Raster vs. vector
cards.body: A raster (or bitmap) image is a fixed grid of pixels, each with its own stored color — like a mosaic of tiny tiles. A vector image instead stores mathematical instructions for shapes, like "draw a circle at this point with this radius and this color."

cards.title: Mosaic tiles vs. a blueprint
cards.body: A raster image is like a mosaic — thousands of fixed colored tiles glued in place. A vector image is like an architect's blueprint — a set of instructions ("wall here, door there") that can be redrawn at any size, always crisp.

cards.title: Photo vs. logo
cards.body: A photo from a phone camera is raster — every pixel captured a real-world color. A company logo made in design software is usually vector — it's stored as circles, lines, and curves, so it can print on a business card or a billboard from the same file.

cards.title: "Vector just means higher quality"
cards.body: Vector isn't automatically "better" — it's a different tool. Vector is great for simple shapes, icons, and text that must resize cleanly. It's a poor fit for a photograph, which has millions of subtle, irregular color variations that shape formulas can't easily describe.

cards.title: Raster vs. Vector
cards.body: How they store and behave when resized:

left.text:
Fixed pixel grid
Blurs/pixelates when enlarged
Best for photos

right.text:
Math shape instructions
Stays crisp at any size
Best for icons/logos

exercises.prompt: Why do machine learning models almost always take raster images as input, not vector images?

options.text: A raster's pixel grid is already a fixed-size grid of numbers, which is what a model can compute on
Vector images are illegal to use for training
Raster images never contain any color information
Vector images can only be viewed on paper

exercises.explain: Exactly — a raster grid of pixel values maps directly onto the numeric arrays models are built to process.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A vector image stores a color value for every single pixel, just like a raster image.
exercises.explain: False. A vector image stores shape formulas (points, curves, fills), not a per-pixel color list — the pixels only appear when it's rendered onto a screen.

exercises.sentence: Enlarging a raster photo a lot causes visible ___, while a vector logo stays sharp at any size.
exercises.explain: Pixelation is correct — stretching a fixed pixel grid makes each tiny square more visible and blocky.

exercises.prompt: A design team needs a company icon that will appear tiny on a phone app AND huge on a highway billboard, without ever looking blurry. Which format should they store the master file in?

options.text: Vector — the shapes redraw cleanly at any size
Raster — pixel grids scale perfectly to any size
It doesn't matter, both scale identically

exercises.explain: Vector is the right call here — the same shape math can be rendered crisp from a tiny icon to a giant billboard.

exercises.prompt: A photographer shoots a portrait, and a graphic designer draws a minimalist star icon for an app. Which format best fits each, and why?

options.text: Raster for the portrait (millions of subtle real-world colors), vector for the icon (simple shapes that must resize cleanly)
Vector for both, since vector is always the more modern choice
Raster for both, since cameras and design software always save the same way
Vector for the portrait and raster for the icon

exercises.explain: Exactly right. Photos capture complex, irregular real-world detail best as a pixel grid; simple geometric icons are cheaper and sharper as shape math. Next: how those pixel grids get squeezed down to a manageable file size.
